runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) – EITC expansion quasi-experiment estimating large participation elasticities for single mothers (~0.7–1.0)
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) – labor supply of single mothers and tax/transfer incentives, participation elasticities around 0.7–1.0
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) – survey of labor supply elasticities noting higher participation elasticities for single mothers relative to other groups
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely recognized as a high-elasticity group at the extensive (participation) margin. The canonical Eissa & Liebman (1996) study of the 1986 EITC expansion found participation elasticities in the range of 0.7–1.0 for this group. Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) similarly find large responses to net wage changes. Relative to prime-age men (elasticities near 0.1–0.2) and married women (~0.3–0.5), single mothers show substantially larger participation responses, largely because many are near the margin of entering the labor force. The medium-run framing allows for some adjustment costs, pushing estimates slightly above immediate short-run responses. My central estimate is 0.7, with meaningful uncertainty on both sides.

The canonical Eissa & Liebman (1996) study of the 1986 EITC expansion found participation elasticities in the range of 0.7\u20131.0 for this group. Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) similarly find large responses to net wage changes. Relative to prime-age men (elasticities near 0.1\u20130.2) and married women (~0.3\u20130.5), single mothers show substantially larger participation responses, largely because many are near the margin of entering the labor force. The medium-run framing allows for some adjustment costs, pushing estimates slightly above immediate short-run responses. My central estimate is 0.7, with meaningful uncertainty on both sides."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.
citations: Eissa, N. & Liebman, J. (1996). Labor supply response to the Earned Income Tax Credit. Quarterly Journal of Economics.

Blundell, R. & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor supply: A review of alternative approaches. Handbook of Labor Economics.
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely regarded as one of the most elastic groups in labor supply, particularly along the extensive (participation) margin. The EITC expansions of the 1980s and 1990s provided large natural experiments. Eissa & Liebman (1996) and Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) estimate participation elasticities in the range of roughly 0.5–1.2 for this group. Blundell & MaCurdy's survey corroborates that extensive-margin elasticities for low-income single parents are substantially higher than for married women or men. A medium-run framing (allowing some adjustment beyond the immediate short run) pushes estimates slightly higher than purely short-run responses. I center my belief around 0.7, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.2 to 1.5.

I center my belief around 0.7, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.2 to 1.5."}

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely documented to have high participation elasticities relative to other demographic groups. The canonical studies exploiting EITC expansions (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) find extensive-margin elasticities on the order of 0.5–1.0 for single mothers. The broader literature review by Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) and subsequent meta-analyses suggest participation elasticities for this group cluster around 0.6–0.9 in the medium run. Uncertainty remains about the exact magnitude, with some studies finding values closer to 0.3–0.5 and others in EITC contexts finding values above 1.0, so I spread the distribution accordingly.

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are a group with historically high participation elasticities, largely because many are near the participation margin rather than adjusting hours continuously. The canonical studies using EITC expansions (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) typically find elasticities in the 0.5–1.0 range for this group in the medium run. Survey articles (Blundell & MaCurdy 1999; Chetty et al. 2013) confirm that participation elasticities for secondary earners and single parents exceed those for primary earners, often centering near 0.5–0.9. I center my belief at 0.7, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.2–1.5 at the 90% credible interval, reflecting variation across identification strategies, time periods, and program contexts.
